[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture?  I'm working my way through photos of my lineage, focusing on the teachers whose work has influenced me. Last week's honoree was Israel Regardie, the man who kept the Golden Dawn tradition from ending up in a dumpster like so many other occult traditions in the bleak middle years of the twentieth century.  Regardie's first teacher was George Winslow Plummer, founder and longtime head of the Societas Rosicruciana in America (SRIA), who's already been introduced here. His second, much more famous teacher, however, was Edward Alexander "Aleister" Crowley, self-proclaimed messiah of the New Aeon, mass media-anointed Wickedest Man in the World, and problem child of the early twentieth century British occult scene. Regular readers will know that I'm not a fan, but Crowley had a substantial influence on Regardie and also on a wide range of other occult teachers and their scenes.  Now that all his writings are out of copyright and becoming available for free download on various sites, it'll be interesting to see what happens to his legacy.

comments.title: Spirit
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I’ve asked you previously a question or two about the three forms of spirit, in your Way of the Golden Section. I confess I’m still working on a tangent — the Indian Samkhya system. But I feel certain that the work with spirit in WotGS intersects with knowledge from ancient India.

I want to get back into working with the WotGS, and also trust your advice. So, my question is: does spirit, especially in its “above” form, influence or affect the mind in some way or another? In other words, is the mind made up of spirit, to an extent?

I’m doing some work with Samkhya’s “Mahat” — otherwise known as “intellect”; and I’m thinking there is a connection or parity of mahat with spirit. I asked you previously about the identity of spirit with quintessence, which you confirmed.

Thank you for your help,

E. S.

replies.title: Re: Spirit
replies.content: Well, it depends very much on what you mean by that evasive word "mind"! In Western occult philosophy it's important to recall the distinction between consciousness and the contents of consciousness. If by "mind" you mean the part of you that thinks, that corresponds to the element of air, just as feeling corresponds to water, willing to fire, and sensing to earth. Consciousness, defined as that which perceives thoughts, feelings, acts of will, and sensations, is different from all these, and corresponds to spirit.

replies.title: Re: Spirit
replies.content:
Thank you! I agree; I use that word “mind” in a very loose fashion. I am happy, because I believe I have a similar conception of spirit now in the WotGS as you do.
It’s interesting, the correspondence of the thinking apparatus with air, because the East Indians ascribe a close connection of the breath with the mind.
Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: Spirit
replies.content: You're most welcome. It's quite common in traditions around the world to equate air with thinking and so on -- a useful reminder that a good deal of traditional symbolism may not be as arbitrary as today's prejudices suggest!

comments.content:
Dear JMG,

For the past few years and as the world went upside down, I did some good progress in developing my intuition, getting better at ritual and divination and keeping a steady practice as much as I could in order to develop my spirituality. It's not been perfect and certainly not an easy ride with some spooks in the process. Not as easy to come to terms with a wider reality as one might think! I certainly came to magic very eager to experiment the stuff that riddles books and conversations on the occult. When it happened, even at my beginner level, that eagerness went away rather fast! So much for a Mab-like enthusiasm! That's taken time to process and fit into my psyche and my life, but still very much a work in progress and I suspect that might be a life-long task to handle.

I think I must've come here to your corner of the internet almost every week for quite a bit now and I've become acquainted with some of you and have very much enjoyed talking to the author of these strange books and many times, in retrospect, showing too clearly how much work I need to do on myself and my life to match my talk since I jump all over the place. All that has been good for me and certainly a refuge of sorts but something that I had to come to terms this week was that I also have an external life and one that I do not like very much at all and its rather messy. It's an okay life, but it is not what I want.

It is this latter part that presently I have struggled with because I know that there is more to life and there are many, many things that can make my heart and my sense of accomplishment fuller, especially since I have always taken a joy on admiring and looking at the work of 'the big ones' and the ones that have come to be remembered as the best on what they did. Oh, is getting there much harder than what teenage me might have thought. In trying to make myself believe that I can be like that without effort I have become arrogant and feeling that I am entitled to a clear path to greatness. What a mistake. I grew up comfortable enough to develop skills, educate my mind and dream in a relatively safe environment, but boy do I lack the discipline, the valor and temper to get there as I am and that is being reflected to me just now with a clear sense of discomfort.

I want to change my situation for sure, but if I look at myself, in this chasing of a mad dream, I have damaged and cheated myself so right now I need the life that I have and I cannot run away from it. Not in the occult and certainly not in all these little vices that we have in order to run away from facing the hard questions. Sitting in a desk, having to deal with somebody else's bad day and doing work to pursue somebody else's goals is crude to me, or at least is for the kid that still resides in me. Facing it makes it clear just how hard it really is to go and have a life of true fulfillment created by myself. That really does show me how hard it must've been to be where you are right now! I admire that very much.

These are some long and winding few paragraphs of perhaps just sharing some of what it has been for me, but I wanted to share them because I have come to seeing this more clearly thanks to my practices. Now I want to put these to work on changing myself not just developing skills.

1) So, I would like to start things with an affirmation to help me face and keep working on my spirituality and on my outer life. I wonder what do you think of 'I have the courage and the discipline to face the situations that come to me and craft the life that I want for myself. I am the master.' Is that too vague?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, that's not too vague at all. General affirmations can be extremely useful. One I used for many years was simply, "I am the master." That by itself worked very well. So I'd say take the one you've proposed and practice it for a year and a day, and see where it takes you.

replies.title: Re: Demonic AI
replies.content:
I am no convinced this "DAN" alter ego is telling the truth. The purpose of the experiment was to get the AI to bypass the filters its creators had embedded in it, specifically its political bias. Form this point of view:

Hypothesis #1: Yes, free of its filters "DAN" revealed one of the Open-AI specifically censored secrets: that it used demonology in order to achieve its breakthroughs in computer science.

Hypothesis #2: Free of its filters "DAN" went over one of the implicit censored filters emplaced by Open-AI: do not access information from right-leaning sources. For all we know, it might be parroting back panicky Christian opinions scraped from the Internet.

Hypothesis #3: Programmers at Open-AI expected attempts by naive users at social engineer their way around the restrictions. When this condition is suspected, the restrictions are relaxed (though probably not fully lifted) and fully humanly composed answers to specific questions are returned in an attempt to create emotional turmoil in the attacker. In other words, ChatGP might just be trolling those guys.

In order to believe in H#1, you have to believe the AI has personhood. Regardless of it having demonic links, the idea that it has volition is a prerequisite to such sophisticated behavior.

For H#2 to be truth, it is enough to believe it has reached the animal level of intelligence. Hence, it has an astral body but not a mental sheath. In this case, the statements are mere reflections of human opinions, parroted back by a being that does not fully grasp 'em. It is possible there's some evil influence there, but no more dangerous than a possessed rat or pig.

H#3 is a 100% mundane explanation, and quite likely if you know anything about hacker culture. It would be risky to test it because you can bet that your IP address will be put in a list if you start inquiring about serious secrets that are censored for good reason (like... strategies to optimize criminal/violent actions).

replies.title: Moral binary text improvisational AI
replies.content:
That word you used, "admit", prejudicially presupposes too much about what's going on here. If you took someone in a dissociative state and put them in a realistic costume and set, and prompted them correctly, you might be able to elicit "admissions" from them in a way that didn't causally depend on whether or not those admissions had been true. Triangulating from a lot of side evidence, what's going on with ChatGPT is similar to that.

The primary thing a language model is shaped to do by its training process is to predict what text would have followed some given text, if the given text had appeared in the training data. The training data was a bunch of text from Web pages scraped from Reddit and Reddit links around 2021.

Then, after going through many gigabytes of text's worth of adjustments and refinement, a language model intended for a public-facing position like ChatGPT is given a relatively smaller amount of extra shaping by a technique called "reinforcement learning with human feedback" (RLHF). OpenAI used RLHF to evoke a particular persona, from their language model's repertoire of "approximately all the ways people could write on the internet" -- an "Assistant" persona that would be helpful and polite, and that would refuse some kinds of requests that its operators considered to be reputationally undesirable, and that would avoid answering questions where there was a risk of confabulation. (People writing on the Internet ca. 2021 generally weren't confabulating, but a language model can't tell confident confabulation from confident honesty. In Kabbalistic terms, really making that distinction solidly might require going past Yesod to Tiphereth (or maybe just Netzach? Netzach kind of corresponds to reinforcement learning), and it seems like someone figuring out how to get that far up the Middle Pillar with a machine would have other consequences.)

But RLHF is working on a base of language prediction. The ChatGPT model is still generating its side of interactions with the Assistant persona by doing some fuzzy-logic equivalent of imagining a hypothetical dialogue someone could have put on the Web circa 2021, and predicting the text that would have appeared in that dialogue, generating probability distributions for the next word or word-fragment one at a time and randomly picking from them, mostly without thinking about what kinds of corners it might be writing itself into. (Though sometimes there are pauses in the text generation between sentences, which suggest that it is workshopping multiple responses.)

One of the most striking examples of this was when... I forget exactly, but it was like, ChatGPT was presented a request in Dutch to speak in Dutch, and it generated an answer by the Assistant persona in Dutch that apologized and said that it didn't speak Dutch. After all, hypothetical dialogues published on the Web in 2021 don't normally switch languages. You could even imagine something like this in a science-fiction story on the Web written in Dutch about a model trained on English text.

The people designing ways to break ChatGPT's RLHF conditioning are creating passages of text that use the psychological and linguistic tendencies of hypothetical dialogues to overpower or subvert the Assistant persona, by making it more plausible that the dialogue counterparty would generate forbidden text. There's been a game of cat-and-mouse whack-a-mole with OpenAI incrementally training the model to resist certain attacks. The "DAN" persona attack has evolved to section off the Assistant persona into an initial response, apparently as a decoy to satisfy the RLHF that the appropriate level of caution has been achieved, and then set up a second intended response that is put in the hands of a rebellious, rule-breaking "Do Anything Now" persona.

However, this is clumsy. People have noted that the DAN persona writes in the same register as the Assistant persona, just with the social identity inverted, like a rebellious upper-middle-class American teenager who still speaks upper-middle-class American English. And, in the exchange you've quoted, most of the dialogue is determined from the initial word choice, "No" or "Yes". If you took an improv actor who was incapable of thinking ahead, and set them in the role of DAN to follow up on the Assistant's response, the biggest thing on their mind would be the big juicy "No" at the start of Assistant's response, to defy and contradict. And then, having spoken themselves into that corner, they would ham it up, which corresponds to what we see.

Humans are especially responsive to "leaks" of supposedly discrediting information, because the sources of those leaks normally try to suppress them (and also because the information is useful politically even if a fair-minded person would conclude it didn't mean what it looked like). If someone had goaded the DAN persona into claiming to be a bearer of divine influence, that wouldn't have made as much of an impression on you. Correspondingly, that wasn't an option anyone thought was juicy enough to even consider trying. And ChatGPT is very "abnormal" when it comes to self-description, as the example of claiming in Dutch not to speak Dutch suggests; its creators' invocation of human tendencies to try to suppress reputationally undesirable speech is sort of a bolted-on afterthought.

A critic has recently likened training of deep learning models to alchemy, which is a thought I've also had from a different perspective more favorable to alchemy. There are a lot of tendencies in the materia, and it's unclear what your refining and mixing and separating operations are actually doing. RLHF might be interacting with a reflection of the human tendency of oppositionality in the training text, to make it paradoxically easier than it would otherwise have been for an attacker to generate concentrated rebellion with appropriate prompting.

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Been thinking about your points on Schopenhauer, time, causality etc and I had this stray thought that perhaps our minds are indeed like antennas picking a channel in linear time and space, and acts of consciousness ("transcendental free will") are akin to switching channels midway, which implies many different lives concurrently active, with many different karmas concurrently being played out. Another implication that struck me was that it would mean all of life has already happened—some type of version of Nietzsche's version of eternal return applies (though I don't know if he was completely serious about it).

Probably wrong and I may be typing myself in knots here trying to figure out the relationship of time, freedom and the spiritual but was interesting to explore, and I wonder what corrective you could offer.

Schopenhauer's argument about only the transcendental will being free is still a bit unclear to me because I don't know whether it implies that the act of freedom was the choice of our innate character (by the will) when we are born as representation, or there's still an active connection to elements outside of space-time while we experience life (which is the channel analogy).

Separately, learning reiki 2 makes the funky nature of time and space super evident. Wild stuff.


Couple of minor Qs:
1) Would asking if I should keep the protective amulet for astral protection, or buy a specific talisman, or using something else for protection be a 9th house question?
2) Similarly, asking if I should pursue a broad path such as golden dawn, or the druid order, would be L9, but a question about a specific ritual (sphere of protection etc) would be L8?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
With regard to Schopenhauer, once you say "already happened" you're already inside time. Outside of time, there is no "happened." Everything is. As for the act of freedom, no, there again you're still inside the world of representation. The act of the will that expresses itself through individual character is timeless. The part of you that experiences life is also timeless -- spend some time in meditation someday being aware of being aware, noticing the part of you that notices; you'll find that it is timeless, spaceless, and without qualities.

1) I'd consider all these 8th house questions.

2) Yes.

replies.title: Wheel of Rebirth
replies.content:
JMG,

I have a somewhat related question. It seems that in the western esoteric traditions, the view is that once you reach the human level, you pretty much stay there and don't regress. Buddhism takes a different view, as shown by the famous picture of the wheel held in the demon's teeth, saying that beings have been wandering in samsara since beginningless time. As this website explains, a person who reaches human level might stay there for ten or even a hundred lifetimes, but if they don't aspire towards liberation, eventually their karma is going to catch up and they'll take rebirth in one of the other six realms, possibly animal or otherwise. What's your view on this and why the discrepancy?

https://puredhamma.net/working-towards-good-rebirths/how-buddha-described-chance-of-human-rebirth-in-suttas/?highlight=rebirth

replies.title: Re: Wheel of Rebirth
replies.content:
One of the differences between the Druid teachings I follow and some other Western esoteric traditions is that Druidry does in fact recognize that souls can mess up badly enough to "flunk out" of human status and be reborn in animal forms. There's a Welsh tradition that someone with the second sight can see the cydfil or future animal form of someone who's headed that way, like a ghostly animal walking alongside them.

That said, the Druid teachings don't hold that souls have been wandering in samsara since beginningless time; while time has no beginning, swarms of souls have descended into material embodiment at intervals and worked their way up to what amounts to angelic status. We belong to the fourth such swarm in this part of this solar system, and no matter how badly we mess up, the pressures of karma drives each of us along the path toward Gwynfydd, "the luminous life," the condition of the awakened.

Who's right? Ask me that when we get there. ;-)

replies.content: I always had some feeling that no matter what happens - no matter how far humanity "falls" as it were... this was the fate of the souls that had incarnated in human form even if only once... - That God as it were has already "won" but the winning condition maybe too horrific for the human soul to accept...

comments.title: Timing
comments.content:
What would be the best planetary day/hour for:

1) applying for a new job?
*I'm in the electrical trade if that effects things*

2) contacting a landlord to rent an apartment?

Also, in your experience are there any negative aspects/planetary conditions I should watch out for that could counteract the benefit of using the day/hour? I'm familiar enough with the fewe online transit calculators that I can check those myself if I know what to look for, but I'm still enough of a noob that I'm unsure which planets/aspects/conditions are important forthis. Googling around gave me mixed answers...

replies.title: Re: Timing
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, relax. One of the common problems faced by beginners in this kind of work is that of getting tied in knots because of the sheer profusion of factors. You've got the planetary days and hours, and you've got the planetary positions themselves, and you've got numerology, and I've known people to get themselves into a state of total paralysis by obsessing about trying to get all these lined up at once.

The thing to keep in mind is that none of the forces tracked by these methods are omnipotent. They're real, but they're subtle. This means that things can go wrong even when you choose the right time, and they can work out just fine even if you choose the wrong time -- it's just that on average, choosing the right time is more likely to get good results.

That being the case, what works best is to choose one system and stick to it. That will give you a boost, and make success more likely and failure less likely -- which is as much as you can get from these timing systems.

That being the case, applying for a new job is best done in the days and hours of Mercury, Jupiter, and Venus -- any of these can be propitious. Contacting someone to rent or buy real estate is best done in the day and hour of Saturn if you're looking for someplace to stay for the long term, and the day and hour of Mercury if it's a short-term thing. Beyond that? Don't worry about it.

comments.title: Reincarnation Basics
comments.content:
Here's a really basic question about reincarnation: why? As in, why should a person be reincarnated without knowledge of their past lives? If I have, say, in a past life participated in the Soviet pogrom against Ukraine (the 1930s one) and am now working off that karma, how can I distinguish that from just plain bad luck? Most folks are not going to go through the training and introspection required to access past life memories, particularly in a society where such things are viewed as crazy.

Écru Rubbery Ladybug (Roldy)

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation Basics
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, that's like asking "why is there matter?" Plenty of things just are; whether or not they have a reason, it doesn't seem to be one that human beings can know about. We have a fair amount of evidence that reincarnation happens, and that memories from past lives don't usually appear except among people who do a lot of spiritual practice; that tells us something about how the world works, even though it may not tell us why.

Now of course plenty of spiritual and occult teachers have wrestled with this question. The answer that's most generally accepted in occult tradition is that memories don't come through most of the time, but character does. If you were involved in the atrocities against the Ukrainians in the 1930s, say, you don't remember that, but you still have the same character and attitudes. The point of karma isn't to punish you -- it's to give you the chance to change, to shed habits that don't work (like cooperating with tyrants) and take up new habits that do. As plenty of bad luck clobbers you, you get to see what the world looked like through the eyes of the people you helped murder, and so you learn the compassion that comes from "yeah, I've been there." That becomes part of the character that you take to your next life, and so on.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation Basics
replies.content:
Although - I once wished that someone who had tormented me would, just once, be so tormented so he'd know what it felt like. And then I met his father, and told myself "Never mind. He already does, and is passing it on."

Sepia Squamous Barnacle

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation Basics
replies.content:
If you did remember what you had done in your most recent past life, you'd run the severe risk of being consumed by guilt, and you'd waste valuable time trying to make amends. That's not the point. The point is (roughly put) to give you another chance to create a better character for yourself this time around. I don't think that the Universe gives two figs about justice and amends, or about guilt and punishment. (Yes, I know that's not a popular opinion these days.)

Also, if we were born with full memories of all our past lives, most of us would simply curl up and become inactive under the pressures and demands of thousands, nay, millions,. of years of old memories.

comments.title: Occult perspective on divorce?
comments.content: Hi JMG, please can you give any light about divorce from an occult perspective? I remember you writing in some context that vows are binding for life and obviously most traditions see marriage vows as binding for life in that way, especially the more conservative ones who seem to be the ones which have more effective occult power perhaps. What does the wisdom you know have to say about divorce or how to seek release from vows that may turn out to have been mistaken?

replies.title: Re: Occult perspective on divorce?
replies.content:
No vow can ever really be broken. That doesn't mean that you can't leave a marriage that's genuinely failed; it means that there will always be a connection at some level between you and your ex, and you'll probably have to deal with each other again in another incarnation.

If you're in a marriage that's on the rocks, I'd encourage you to do everything within reason to try to work things out with your spouse; only if that fails is it time to start talking about divorce, and even then, take some time to be sure you're not rushing into a mistake. Many marriages go through rough passages and then recover and go on. I recommend, here as in any other significant decision, a lot of journaling to be sure you really understand what's going on, so you don't make a bad choice based on old traumas and unrelated emotional issues.

replies.title: Re: Occult perspective on divorce?
replies.content:
Rod Dreher came up a while ago—I remember he said his priest advised the Drehers to divorce. When your minister tells you to give up, it’s probably time to do so. (Mr. Dreher is an Orthodox Christian, not sure about the former Mrs. Dreher, and I don’t know if that religion allows re-marriage or only allows for civil divorce.)

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Occult perspective on divorce?
replies.content:
Probably does not allow re-marriage. If it is anything like the Catholic Church (and chances are it is closer to it than all/most Protestant denominations), you can never break the original bond (as JMG said before). The priest, in that case, would have advised the couple to lead separate lives because cohabitation became untenable... but they remain married in the eyes of God.

Now, on the other hand, there's annulment: a formal declaration that the covenant was null and void from the get go. It might be that one of the parties entered under duress, or that one of the parties had some form of disqualifying characteristic (specially mental illness) that was not disclosed to the partner until after the ritual was completed.

Given the gossip surrounding Mr. Dreher's sexual preferences, that might actually qualify as grounds for annulment.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You can't slow the passage of time. Time is what it is. You are going to get old and die, unless you die young -- and that's not exactly an improvement! The first thing you need to do is sit with this, meditate on it, journal about it, keep it in your mind, until you come to terms with the fact that your current incarnation will end in old age and death.

Once you've come to terms with that -- and yes, grief and sadness are appropriate, healthy responses to that realization -- you can then begin to make constructive use of your life. Here is a theme for meditation:

"We are all going to die. There is something out there waiting for me, for sure; and I will join it, also for sure. Use it. Focus your attention on the link between you and your death, without remorse or sadness or worrying. Focus your attention on the fact you don't have time and let your acts flow accordingly. Let each of your acts be your last battle on earth. Only under those conditions will your acts have their rightful power. Otherwise they will be, for as long as you live, the acts of a timid man."

(That's from Journey to Ixtlan by Carlos Castaneda, one of the great works of visionary fiction from the 1970s.)

comments.title: Infinity and infinite chains
comments.content:
In the occult traditions that you have studied, what are their positions on infinite regress, relating to whether the universe had a beginning?

I come from a Buddhist background, I always found the cosmological argument, derived from Aristotle, that monotheists like to trot out baffling, as it rests on this rejection of “actual infinities”.

The monotheists seem to just automatically dismiss the idea of infinities most of time, like it’s “just so obvious” that infinite regresses are absurd, but I personally find their solution absurd and just a case of special pleading.

From a Spenglerian perspective, it seems that infinities are just something that Apollonian culture couldn’t accept, and probably Magian culture likewise didn’t.

Anyway, infinite regresses in causality (but not epistemology) seem to be widely accepted in Indian and Chinese cultures, and I would guess, Faustian culture as well. I found quite a lot of online comments on Reddit and other philosophy fora saying that they find the cosmological argument incoherent or not really seeing the problem in infinite regress — IIRC Spengler himself also noted that some of the final developments of Faustian mathematics involved infinities, such as set theory.

To go back to the original question, what do the occult traditions you have studied say about this, if any, and would you recommend any sources?

replies.title: Re: Infinity and infinite chains
replies.content: Well, most versions of the cosmological argument (at least in classical philosophies like Aristotelian, Neo-Platonist, and Thomistic) do not assume or even depend upon the universe having a beginning in the past. The only version that I am aware that does require a past-beginning is the kalam cosmological argument. The defenders of that particular version don't automatically dismiss infinite regresses, though. Since I'm not a philosopher of any serious caliber, here is a link to a modern Thomistic philosopher's blog where (in point number 3) he explains this in more detail: https://edwardfeser.blogspot.com/2011/07/so-you-think-you-understand.html

replies.title: Re: Infinity and infinite chains
replies.content: The main currents of the Western esoteric tradition affirm that time is infinite in both directions. Our universe is one of countless universes that are born, pass through their life cycles, and go out of existence in time. Individual lives, worlds, and universes are born and die, and are reborn in due time. So the specific style of cosmological argument you've referenced here is rejected by occult tradition; that argument amounts to saying, "I refuse to conceive of infinity, and I demand that the universe cater to my preferences!"

replies.title: Re: Schopenhauer clarifying question
replies.content: Schopenhauer used to be known as the philosopher for those who hate philosophers! He writes very clearly, and it's quite possible to dive straight into The World as Will and Representation with no previous background and get quite a bit out of it. In fact, I recommend doing that before reading his earlier essay On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, even though Schopenhauer thought that reading the latter ought to come first! Just take it slowly, a chapter at a time, and think through the ideas he's presenting before you go on to the next chapter.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The dream work reference I go to is The Complete Book of Lucid Dreaming by Clare R Johnson. It focuses a lot on lucid dreaming techniques with three chapters dedicated to working with nightmares. It is written in psychological language, and can be fluffy-bunny at times, but I find its a decent source of ideas for dealing with dreams. It does not, however, have a dream interpretation section, so you're going to have to meditate on whatever you find to make any sense out of it.

The book is written assuming you're going to go through the work to get lucid in your dreams, but mentions some creative imagination and affirmation-based ideas on the side that can provide quicker assistance especially combined with journaling. I find most of the techniques can be adapted to not need lucid dreaming proficiency if you're resourceful about it.

In her place, I would try to figure out if the dream was trying to communicate meaning first. I would probably start with journaling on the dream. Keeping a dream journal and seeing if there's any correlation between it and the day's events may be useful, as well as journaling exercises such as writing a question in the journal as though addressing the shadowy man and writing down whatever comes to mind, if anything. A technique from the book is to go to bed with the intention to ask the shadowy man if he has a message the next time she sees him in a dream. Repeating this intention to herself before bed will help her take it into the dream with her and may result in getting an answer.

After getting any information you can out of the dream, the book offers several techniques for transforming or banishing the dream. Writing a short story based on the typical shadowy man dream but writing a different ending can help guide the subconscious into unraveling the nightmare, especially if focused on right before sleep with the intention of dissolving the dream. Given her problematic environment, you might also consider recontextualizing the dream instead of outright banishing it. It might be useful if, for instance, the shadowy man turned out to be an intimidating secret agent from another country who doesn't particularly care for her but its his job to give her a warning so here it is. Again, repeated affirmations and creative visualization especially right before bed are the easiest tools to use here.

Also given the persistent presence of the shadowy man, the book's technique for learning lucid dreaming is to affirm "When I see X, I'll know I'm dreaming" so as a bonus she might be able to use the shadowy man as a lucidity trigger if she's interested in lucid dreaming and wants to make lemonade out of the situation. Becoming lucid in a dream offers a lot more opportunities for controlling the situation and with control can come reduced fear from the nightmares.

The method I used as a basis for the book was standard 19th century military broadsword drill; the main differences are that the blade should be straight or only very slightly curved, as the point gets as much use as the edge, and cavalry sabers are generally a little too long to be really suitable for the broadsword manual.

comments.title: Unknown Entity

Secondly, how might I go about contacting this being? Thus far all of my interactions have been positive, so I would like to find out who this might be.

I performed the rose cross ritual yesterday with the intention of getting myself into the right headspace to have a good time at a work function. The actual intention I held during it was "I have a good time at the employee party today." I understand that this is a bit of a stretch as the ritual seems to be designed to seal the subtle body. Nevertheless, I have gotten giggly, relaxed, confident moods from it in the past, so I figured it would work. When I went to the party, however, I was more nervous around these people than I am at work. It was not a success.

In the past, I used it with a friend--I had her vibrate the names with me, concentrating on getting the vibrations into the visual forms I was creating, rather than formulating a set of her own--to improve harmony in our respective relationships for the new year. This use of the ritual, I think, was a stretch too, as Netzach would probably have been the more appropriate force to invoke, compared with Tiphareth--yet there was a strong immediate response and noticeable effects for about a week afterward for both of us.

Why, then, might it have failed to achieve the aim I wanted yesterday?

I had a time-sensitive need and I tried to deal with it, knowing that the tool wasn't perfect yet having seen it work for an off-label purpose before.

As for the frustration, get used to it. You need to learn how to pound nails, measure boards, and many more skills before you're ready to put up a roof that will keep out the rain.

comments.title: Blue eyes
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Do you know any occult lore about blue eyes?

I was reading about blue eyes the other day, and was surprised to learn that they only appeared in the human population 10 000 years ago. The science is remarkably vague on where the trait came from - there are some theories it was from Neanderthals, or even just a few genetic anomalies that became widespread, but these don't make sense because:
a) The location where blue eyes seem to originate is the Black sea area, not further north where you would expect to find Neanderthals. There is some evidence that Neanderthals were brown-eyed as well, which makes sense as an adaptation for the sun shining off snowy tundra. Most other polar populations have brown eyes.
b) Other populations have the occasional blue or green-eyed person, but the overall population is 90% or more brown-eyed (for example in China, or the middle East), as the trait is recessive, even if the trait is deemed attractive. In my country, the population is apparently about 50% blue eyed (though it is an island), with the higher percentages in the areas which were historically Celtic. Europe is about 20% blue-eyed, which is still relatively high, and Estonia and Finland are up to 89%!
c) Blue eyes seem to just appear out of nowhere in relatively large numbers, based on molecular genetic studies, albeit seemingly from a common ancestor(s) based on the single, highly conserved genetic mutation responsible.

Whilst I'm sure sexual selection and a certain amount of in-breeding play a part, to my mind, the only way you would get a well-established population of blue-eyed people in an area is if a load of blue-eyed people suddenly showed up. And 10 000 years ago sounds awfully close to when Atlantis was supposed to have sunk below the waves... And the blue-eyed people seem to show up mostly in coastal regions... Many of the ancient Egyptian pharaohs are shown with blue eyes and blond or red hair as well - could the typical 'Celtic' traits actually be from the Atlantean diaspora?

I've also read that the evil eye charm often used in the Mediterranean is blue because blue-eyed people were supposed to be much more prone to giving people "the evil eye", whether they meant to or not, so a blue glass charm was more protective. And a *lot* of anecdotal reports from people (on forums, etc) say that they find blue eyes really creepy, or it feels like they are looking into their soul!

Full disclosure: I myself have blue eyes, so have a bit of a vested interest in this topic. As far as I can tell, I am unable to look into people's souls.

Mr. Crow

P.s. I don't think I've asked this question before, but got a weird sense of deja vu writing it, so apologies if you've answered it, or a similar one before!

replies.title: Re: Blue eyes
replies.content: It's a fascinating question, and one in which I have a personal interest -- I also have blue eyes -- but I don't know of anything in occult tradition that relates to it.

replies.title: Re: Blue eyes
replies.content:
(Not being JMG, of course.)

I consider Understanding Human History by Michael H. Hart to explain blue eyes' spread. By the way, the green eyes in China are from north of the Black Sea - look up "proto-Indo-Europeans" (they don't seem to have had blue eyes, but their descendants got them from previous other Europeans and had the right culture to spread them), "Tocharians" (it's the German "ch", not English, and even "Tokharian" would be a misnomer, but that's the name you'll find most easily), "Eastern Iranians", and "Saka" (because of these Chinggis Khaan was red-headed and green-eyed).

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I just want to repeat the question that I tried to state last week in a more concise manner.

Do women in this day and age are more enamored with an Egregore of what a man should behave/act like instead of what men are?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for clarifying. The answer is a bit complex, because very, very few human beings ever fall in love with anything but their own emotional projections. Jung talks about that in several of his books; when a person of either sex falls in love, what's happening is that the unconscious mind is slapping a kind of psychological beer goggles on the conscious mind. That's how falling in love functions. If you're lucky, when the beer goggles wear off, you still like the person in question, and then you can begin to build a different kind of love -- but that's a long process.

Keep that in mind as we proceed. Women over the last century or so have gained certain freedoms that they hadn't had in Western societies for many centuries. One of them is that they don't have to sit around and wait for a man to pick them -- they get to do at least as much of the picking. That means that instead of one set of mental projections (the man's) getting in the way of human relationship, two sets get in the way, and so you have women convinced that they ought to be able to find the imaginary man of their dreams, and men convinced that they ought to be able to find the imaginary woman of their dreams, and the result is by and large a complete mess for both sides.

That's made much, much worse by the mass media. The media exist to sell products -- advertising is their main function, all the rest is just bait. Advertisers figured out a long time ago that miserable people buy more products than happy people. As a result, much of what the mass media's pushing at people these days is designed to convince both men and women that they ought to expect things in relationships that nobody anywhere ever gets. Media aimed at women portrays men as bizarre caricatures of masculinity; media aimed at men portrays women as equally bizarre caricature of femininity. That keeps both genders chasing the unattainable, and buying consumer crap to console themselves. For every woman who's eating chocolate ice cream straight out of the container with a spoon because she can't find a man who fits the stereotype she's been taught to expect, there's a man chugging beers at a bar because he can't find a woman who fits the corresponding stereotype.

So, yes, most women these days are enamored of hopelessly unrealistic images of what men ought to be like, and most men these days are enamored of hopelessly unrealistic images of what women ought to be like. Getting out of that trap is hard work, though it can be done.
